flashcards.back: A specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data so that it can be accessed and updated efficiently.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between linear and non-linear data structures?
flashcards.back: In linear structures (array, stack, queue, linked list), elements are arranged sequentially. In non-linear structures (tree, graph), elements can be connected to multiple elements.

flashcards.front: What is an Abstract Data Type (ADT)?
flashcards.back: A data type defined by its behavior and operations rather than its implementation. Examples include Stack ADT (push, pop, peek) and Queue ADT (enqueue, dequeue, front).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between static and dynamic data structures?
flashcards.back: Static structures (arrays) have fixed size at compile time. Dynamic structures (linked lists) can grow or shrink during runtime.
A measure of the amount of time an algorithm takes as a function of input size, expressed in Big-O notation (e.g., O(1), O(n), O(log n)).

flashcards.front: Name the common operations on data structures.
flashcards.back: Traversing, Searching, Insertion, Deletion, Sorting, Merging, and Updating.

flashcards.front: What is LIFO and which data structure uses it?
flashcards.back: LIFO stands for Last In First Out. The Stack data structure follows this principle where the last element added is the first to be removed.

flashcards.front: What is FIFO and which data structure uses it?
flashcards.back: FIFO stands for First In First Out. The Queue data structure follows this principle where the first element added is the first to be removed.

flashcards.front: What are the criteria for selecting a data structure?
flashcards.back: Type of data to be stored, operations to be performed, memory constraints, time complexity requirements, and whether dynamic or static sizing is needed.
A measure of the amount of memory an algorithm uses as a function of input size, including both the space for input data and auxiliary space.
